name: Mae Nak Phra Khanong
alias: Nang Nak · the wife who would not leave
body: First it is the sight of an ordinary house lit warm against the canal dark, a woman at the doorway pounding rice in a wooden mortar, a child balanced on her hip, the smell of cooking and woodsmoke drifting low over the water as though nothing in the world were wrong. Travellers poling along the Phra Khanong klong slow their boats to admire it, the lamplight on the boards, the small domestic music of a household at its evening. Only later, recounting the night to others, do they grow uneasy and remember the things the eye had let pass: that no man was ever seen at that house, that the lamps burned long after the rest of the village had gone dark and silent, and that the woman herself never once stepped beyond her own threshold into the open light of the road, but stayed always framed in the doorway as if the doorway were the edge of the world she was permitted.

Nak died in childbirth while her husband Mak was away at the war, and she would not accept it. Love held her in the shape of a living wife: she kept the house, cooked the meals, carried the child, and waited, and when Mak came home wounded and weary she greeted him at the landing as though he had never been gone a single day. The tell, when it comes, is domestic and terrible in its smallness. One evening, grinding chillies for nam phrik on the raised floor of the house, she lets a lime slip and roll through a gap between the boards, and rather than rise and go down for it she simply reaches, and her arm follows the fruit down, stretching long and pale and boneless through the gap to the bare earth below to gather it back. To watch a wife reach where no living arm can reach is to understand at last whose roof has sheltered you, and to know that the bargain she offers so gently, a home that never ends and a love that never tires, is one no living man can survive accepting.

What lingers is the patience of her devotion, a hunger wearing the soft clothes of tenderness, the sense that to be loved by the dead is its own slow kind of drowning. They say her rage, once her secret was out, scattered the villagers screaming across the rice fields, and that no charm or fence of thorns availed until a holy man came at last to bind her and quiet the river road. To this day the faithful do not come in fear. They bring her cosmetics and folded silk dresses and small toys for the child she never raised to walk, and lay them at the shrine attributed to her at Wat Mahabut in Phra Khanong, where rows of those dresses hang on either side and the incense never quite stops burning, and where women still kneel before dawn on the night before the lottery draw to ask the most loyal ghost in Bangkok for a number, or for luck in love.
note: The classic ending has the monk Somdet Phra Phutthachan (To Phromrangsi) subduing Nak and confining her spirit, in the best-known version by inscribing her power into a piece of her own skull bone worn as a waistband relic. Wards in the wider tradition are the standard ones against a malevolent ghost (phi): the prayers and sacred thread of an ordained monk, sanctified water, and yantra cloth. The tale is firmly local to the Phra Khanong district of Bangkok and is retold endlessly in Thai film, song, and theatre.

Whether Nak ever lived is unsettled; the story is usually dated to the reign of Rama IV or V in the nineteenth century. The shrine at Wat Mahabut remains an active site of devotion, where supplicants ask favours of a ghost remembered less for terror than for refusing to stop loving.

recountings.text: Visiting the shrine inside Wat Mahabut, journalist Laurel Tuohy found it less frightening than expected and more strange: rows of traditional Thai dresses hang on both sides, sold on-site so that worshippers whose wishes were granted can buy one and return it as thanks. She describes a tulle-wrapped tree shrine, golden statues, burning incense, and a TV running a 24-hour live feed of the shrine itself, since Mae Nak is said to enjoy watching. This is a specific writer's first-person visit account, not general lore.

Time Out Bangkok describes the shrine as a small wooden structure beside the canal in the Wat Mahabut complex, drawing pregnant women hoping for an easy birth, young men wanting to dodge the military draft, and crowds chasing lucky lottery numbers. On the night before a lottery draw the temple stays open until dawn, with candles everywhere and fortune tellers lining the alley, and devotees rub the bark of the giant Ton Takien tree hoping numbers will appear. This is a widely circulated description of standard pilgrim devotion at the shrine, not a single named person's experience.

A commonly retold debunking version, recorded on Wikipedia from research by Thai historian Anek Nawikamul, traces the tale to an 1899 newspaper article and proposes that the real figure was a pregnant woman, Amdaeng Nak, who died in childbirth. In this account her grieving son allegedly invented the ghost to keep his father from remarrying, dressing as a woman and throwing rocks at passing boats to keep the legend alive. This is widely circulated historical lore rather than a personal sighting.
recountings.attribution: Wikipedia, Mae Nak Phra Khanong (citing historian Anek Nawikamul)

references.title: Mae Nak: Visiting the weird, wonderful shrine dedicated to Thailand's famous ghost
